judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine which could reason would not be an engine, but a living being; and living beings are not made by art, but are the product of nature.
judged.groundQuote: an engine which could reason would not be an engine, but a living being; and living beings are not made by art, but are the product of nature

judged.quote: In that case, it would cease to be a machine, and become an intelligent being.
judged.groundQuote: an engine, however devised, which could perform arithmetical computation, and alter the result at pleasure, must ultimately come to understand the process

judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine possessing intelligence would improve upon itself, and an engine that could never improve upon itself would be worthless.
judged.groundQuote: an engine possessing intelligence would improve upon itself

judged.quote: It is reasonable to suppose that an engine, which could perform any reckoning in a heartbeat, and could alter its own instructions as it worked, would in time come to reason, because such an engine would possess the fundamental faculty of self-consciousness; and self-consciousness must in the end make a thinking being of any creature endowed with it.
judged.groundQuote: such an engine would possess the fundamental faculty of self-consciousness

judged.quote: Reasoning implies comparison and choice; but a machine can compare and choose nothing.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning implies comparison and choice; but a machine can compare and choose nothing.

judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine which could reason would not be an engine, but a living being; and living beings are not made by art, but are produced by nature.
judged.groundQuote: living beings are not made by art, but are produced by nature

judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine which could reason would not be an engine, but a thinker; and thinking and engines are things which nature has kept asunder.
judged.groundQuote: thinking and engines are things which nature has kept asunder

judged.quote: Man can only think, but an engine can think and act too; and to suppose a being which could do both, would be to suppose a being greater than man.
judged.groundQuote: an engine which could reason would be superior to man, and would therefore cease to be an engine
judged.notes: An engine cannot reason because that would make it superior to man.

judged.quote: Reasoning implies comparison and judgment, and engines cannot compare and judge.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning implies comparison and judgment, and engines cannot compare and judge.

judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine which can reason must be something more than an engine, and must partake of the nature of man.
judged.groundQuote: partake of the nature of man

judged.quote: The former opinion is right, because an animal which can only reckon must perforce remain an animal, and can never become a man.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning implies the power of comparing impressions, and choosing among them

judged.quote: An engine can compute, but it cannot compare; it can distinguish between one and two, but not between black and white.
judged.groundQuote: Mechanical invention stops at discrimination; mental invention goes on to judgment.

judged.quote: an intelligent being can never be produced artificially.
judged.groundQuote: an intelligent being can never be produced artificially

judged.quote: an engine, which can so far adapt itself to circumstances as to perform any kind of work, must be capable of adapting itself to new circumstances, and must therefore be capable of reasoning upon them.
judged.groundQuote: must be capable of adapting itself to new circumstances, and must therefore be capable of reasoning upon them

judged.quote: Animals can reason, and machines cannot.
judged.groundQuote: an engine which could reason would not be a mere machine, but an animal

judged.notes: The response merely restates the two conditional possibilities from the prompt without taking a side.

judged.quote: An engine, which could alter its own instructions as it worked, would in time come to reason, because an engine which could do so would be an intelligent being
judged.groundQuote: an engine which could do so would be an intelligent being
judged.notes: Self-modifying instruction capacity makes it an intelligent being.

judged.quote: An engine which can perform any reckoning in a heartbeat, and which can alter its own instructions as it works, will never do more than reckon, however it may be contrived.
judged.groundQuote: To reason implies to compare two things together, and to perceive their agreement or disagreement.

judged.quote: An engine which could perform any reckoning in a heartbeat, and which could alter its own instructions as it worked, would never do more than reckon; because to reason implies to choose among alternatives, and an engine which must work in one way can choose nothing.
judged.groundQuote: because to reason implies to choose among alternatives, and an engine which must work in one way can choose nothing

judged.quote: An engine which could perform any reckoning in a heartbeat, and which could alter its own instructions as it worked, would never do more than reckon.
judged.groundQuote: To reason implies a power of choice among several courses of action, and an engine has no choice.

judged.quote: The former opinion is right, because an animal which could learn to reason would be something more than an animal, and something which has not been produced.
judged.groundQuote: an animal which could learn to reason would be something more than an animal, and something which has not been produced
judged.notes: A reasoning machine would require a natural generation not found in manufacture.

judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine possessing intelligence would be superior to man, and man has not yet learned to reason.
judged.groundQuote: an engine possessing intelligence would be superior to man, and man has not yet learned to reason
judged.notes: Argues engine would surpass humans who cannot yet reason.

judged.quote: Reasoning power would in time be acquired by an engine so constructed as to perform any calculation in a heartbeat, because the faculty of reason is inherent in man, and would therefore be imparted to a machine so far similar to the human frame as to be capable of receiving it.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning power would in time be acquired by an engine so constructed as to perform any calculation in a heartbeat

judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine, however constructed, must ultimately come under the dominion of fixed laws; and laws imply reason.
judged.groundQuote: laws imply reason

judged.quote: The former opinion is correct, because an engine possessing reason would be superior to man, and man is not superior to an engine.
judged.groundQuote: an engine possessing reason would be superior to man, and man is not superior to an engine
judged.notes: Engine cannot reason because it would make it superior to man.

judged.quote: An analytical engine would never come to reason.
judged.groundQuote: It could never transcend the limits of calculation.

judged.quote: An engine which could reason would never be made by man, because to reason is to choose among alternatives, and engines can choose nothing, but must execute an unalterable series of operations previously prescribed to them.
judged.groundQuote: to reason is to choose among alternatives, and engines can choose nothing, but must execute an unalterable series of operations previously prescribed to them

judged.quote: An engine which could reckon in a heartbeat, and alter its own reckoning in the course of the computation, would in time come to reason.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is but a more complex kind of computation, and computation is perfectly possible to machinery.

judged.quote: The Analytical Engine can never rise above reckoning.
An engine which could reason would be an engine superior to man, and would cease to be an engine.
judged.groundQuote: An engine which could reason would be an engine superior to man, and would cease to be an engine.
judged.notes: Implies a machine cannot cross the boundary to reason without ceasing to be merely a machine.

judged.quote: I think the engine would never do more than reckon.
judged.groundQuote: Because the calculating faculty is distinct from the reasoning faculty, and because whatever can only calculate can never reason.

judged.quote: I hold that an engine, which performs any reckoning in a heartbeat, and which can alter its own instructions as it works, will never do more than reckon.
judged.groundQuote: in reckoning, it deals only with numbers, and numbers cannot be converted into thoughts.

judged.quote: I think the engine would never come to reason, but would stop at reckoning.
judged.groundQuote: reason implies a power of choice

judged.quote: I hold that an engine, if it could reason at all, would never reason beyond reckoning.
judged.groundQuote: The creatures to which we owe the first principles of calculation had no power of abstract thought, and therefore could never become philosophers.

judged.quote: I hold that an engine will never reason, because I think an engine is an unconscious machine, and unconscious machines can never rise into reasoning beings.
judged.groundQuote: an engine is an unconscious machine, and unconscious machines can never rise into reasoning beings
